name: Zenon
classification_notes: Zenon draws from Renaissance proportions and chancery italic traditions, with letterforms developed from analysis of 16th-century masterpieces including Robert Granjon's italics, placing it firmly in the humanist/Renaissance serif tradition.
summary: A book typeface by Riccardo Olocco rooted in Renaissance proportions, with letterforms derived from 16th-century masters and a global 2-degree slope applied across all glyphs for subtle organic rhythm.
description: Developed from close analysis of Renaissance typography and Robert Granjon's italic models, Zenon moderates its historical sources towards even modern letter widths while retaining the warmth and calligraphic logic of its origins. The italic is a narrow chancery cut sloped at 9 degrees, drawn independently from the roman rather than mechanically obliqued, with deep junctions that give it a lively, spiky character. Eight weights from Book to Black, each with a matching italic, are available as static fonts and variable font files, with variable contrast that shifts from low in lighter weights to pronounced in heavier ones.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Zenon as a book typeface for print and screen designed by Riccardo Olocco, first released in 2013 and extended to eight weights in 2021. The roman derives from Renaissance typographic masterpieces, with letterforms gradually condensed toward modern proportions and all glyphs sloped by approximately 2 degrees for an organic quality. The chancery italic was drawn from analysis of Robert Granjon's italics and 16th-century calligraphers, remaining independent of the roman in its construction while matching it in general measurements.

name: AM Appennino
classification_notes: A bold, low-contrast, fully cursive script with all letters joined, revived from a 1938 Italian wood type; its combination of round strokes, straight diagonals and sharp corners places it firmly in the connected script category.
summary: A digital revival of a 1938 Italian wood type by Xilografia di Verona, reconstructed by Valentina Casali and Marco Goran Romano with refined spacing and redrawn problem letters.
description: Originally released as a wood type around 1938, the face blends geometric and vernacular forms, using straight diagonal upper strokes on letters such as M, N, m and n in place of the more typical curved entry strokes. Casali and Romano addressed the rough inconsistencies of the source material — uneven contrast, awkward diagonal cuts, uniform joining strokes — redesigning several letters entirely for the digital environment. OpenType features include initial and final forms and an all-caps stylistic set for uppercase-only settings.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe AM Appennino as a bold, low-contrast, script typeface in which all letters are joined. Unlike AM Majella, it is not entirely geometric and its letters are fully cursive. The revival draws on a wood type issued by Xilografia di Verona around 1938, which featured a distinctive mix of geometric and vernacular forms, and was refined by the designers to improve legibility and achieve even spacing across letter combinations.

classification_notes: A display typeface with a single heavy weight and condensed widths, reviving the eccentric letterforms of 1970s phototypesetting and the psychedelic rock poster tradition. Its exaggerated forms and ornamental character set it firmly in the display category.
summary: A single-weight display revival by Giulio Galli drawing on 1970s phototypesetting and psychedelic rock poster lettering, available in four widths including a variable font.
description: Inspired by Eightball, a typeface published by Visual Graphic Corporation in the 1970s, and the hand-lettered eccentricities of psychedelic rock posters from the mid-1960s onwards, this typeface exists in one heavy weight stretched across four widths: Heavy, SemiCondensed, Condensed and ExtraCondensed. A variable font version is also included. Ornamental alternates, swash characters and stylistic sets extend the expressive range well beyond what a single weight might suggest.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe 8 as a 'revivalish' typeface designed for display and branding, its name alluding to phototypesetting and the infinity symbol. Designed by Giulio Galli and developed between 2021 and 2025, it takes inspiration from the VGC Eightball typeface and the quirky letterforms of the psychedelic rock scene since the mid-1960s.

name: Alleanz Sans
classification_notes: An ultra-light display grotesque with pronounced thick-thin contrast, wide letterforms and deliberately eccentric details that push it firmly into display territory rather than functional text use.
summary: An ultra-light display grotesque by Erasmo Ciufo that draws on sci-fi cinema and futurist aesthetics, with eccentric letterforms and four stylistic sets of alternate characters.
description: Wide proportions and a pronounced thick-thin contrast give Alleanz Sans an otherworldly, high-contrast silhouette suited to headlines and title sequences. Unusual details throughout — a central bar on the g that overruns the bowl, f and t terminals that curl beyond convention — are extended by four stylistic sets offering lexigram-style alternates. The single ExtraLight weight positions it as a specialist display tool for fashion, technology and speculative branding contexts.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Alleanz Sans as a scream — an ultra-light display grotesque that fuses fashion with science fiction, technology and branding. Designed by Erasmo Ciufo and released in 2020, it revisits futurism through generous proportions, swelling curved strokes and radical letterforms, with the design partly inspired by a Hollywood screenwriter's unfinished screenplay and the imagined typeface system within it.

name: AM Aosta
classification_notes: A monolinear geometric sans revival of a pre-war Italian wood type. The eccentric capital forms, with letters extending above and below conventional bounds, push it firmly into display territory despite the underlying geometric construction.
summary: A digital revival of a pre-war Italian wood type from Xilografia di Verona, notable for its eccentric capitals that extend both above the cap height and below the baseline.
description: Originally issued as a wood type shortly before World War 2, AM Aosta is distinguished by capitals taller than the ascenders, with certain letters such as D, P and Z sitting below the baseline. Designers Stefano Baldassari and James D. Barranger refined the sometimes crude original construction, reduced the extreme proportions for usability and added three stylistic sets for underlined, overlined and double-lined letterforms, along with a connected caps system designed for display work. The result is a single-weight family with genuine historical grounding and a decorative range unusual in any geometric sans.
foundry_blurb: CAST describes AM Aosta as a monolinear geometric sans with an extensive character set, featuring capitals that extend above the ascenders and in some cases below the baseline, and including OpenType features for underlines, overlines, combined double lines, small caps and connected cap forms designed for display-oriented use. They note it as a digital revival of a wood type originally released by Xilografia di Verona before World War 2.

name: Arkit
classification_notes: Described by CAST as a constructivist sans with humanist qualities; its geometric structure is tempered by slightly tapered strokes and an organic rhythm that place it between the geometric and humanist sans traditions.
summary: A geometric sans by Erasmo Ciufo with quietly organic details, spanning ten weights from Hairline to the display-focused Bomb.
description: Designed between 2012 and 2019, it pairs a constructed, geometric silhouette with subtly tapered strokes that soften its mechanical qualities. A large x-height makes it a practical choice for continuous text and small-scale setting, while the heaviest cuts — ExtraBold, Heavy and Bomb — are conceived explicitly for signage and headline work. The ten-weight range is notably broad for a single-width family with no italic complement.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Arkit as a constructivist sans with a humanist taste, its geometric appearance concealing an organic soul expressed through slightly tapered strokes. They note that a big x-height makes it well-suited to book setting at body and caption sizes, while the upper weights including ExtraBold, Heavy and Bomb are conceived specifically for signage and display applications.

name: Arzachel
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a humanist sanserif; organic letter forms, flared stem terminations, inscriptional capitals and low-to-moderate contrast all point firmly to the humanist sans tradition.
summary: A humanist sans by Riccardo Olocco with flared stem terminations, gently sloped romans and inscriptional capitals that hold up equally well at text and display sizes.
description: Designed to companion Zenon and first used in print while still in development, Arzachel carries several deliberate details: romans that are lightly sloped across all styles, letter terminals that widen outward, and capitals with a quiet inscriptional quality. Contrast stays low in lighter weights but becomes pronounced in the Black. Four weights with matching italics give publishers and editorial designers a compact but characterful range.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Arzachel as a humanist sanserif with a large x-height and organic character, originally conceived to accompany Zenon at small sizes. They note it is scientifically sharp at text sizes yet rugged and dramatic in headlines, and highlight details such as gently sloped romans, flared upper stem terminations, widening terminals and inscriptional capitals. It has appeared in publications including James Clough's Signs of Italy and Eye magazine's Pulp supplement for Fedrigoni.

name: AM Assab
classification_notes: An all-caps, counterless geometric sans built from circles, triangles and rectangles, making it a display face in the constructivist/modernist tradition rather than a conventional text geometric sans.
summary: A digital revival of a 1930s Italian wood type, reconstructed from the counterless geometric sans tradition that ran through Fascist-era advertising and into Milton Glaser's Baby Teeth.
description: Originally produced by Xilografia Ruggero Zuliani & C. in Verona in the early 1930s, Assab belongs to a strain of counterless sanserifs that shaped Italian advertising typography and found expression in Fortunato Depero's experimental lettering. Alessandro Bombieri's revival keeps close to the wooden source while rectifying rough edges and adding an alternate S to prevent the overlaps inherent in letter combinations such as ST and LS. A single Regular weight covers an extensive Latin character set spanning over seventy languages.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe AM Assab as an all-caps geometric sans of elementary construction, built from circles, triangles and rectangles with almost no contrast and no counters, retaining only open counters as in C and E. The digital revival by Alessandro Bombieri stays remarkably close to the original wood type, with letterforms rectified to remove roughness and a new alternate S added to resolve spacing issues that were imperceptible in wood type printing but visible digitally.

name: AM Augusta
classification_notes: An all-caps inline geometric revival with a characteristic shaded grid construction, placing it firmly in the display category. The hybrid of late-1920s German inline conventions with residual Art Nouveau letterforms makes it distinct from a standard geometric sans.
summary: A digital revival of a 1930s Neapolitan metal type, combining geometric inline construction with residual Art Nouveau letterforms in a single all-caps weight.
description: Designed by Riccardo Olocco, this revival traces back to the Fonderia Tipografica Meridionale Armando De Luca of Naples, a foundry active from 1896 until the early 1980s. Each character pairs a solid side with a shaded side built from a grid of thin parallel lines, a construction technique shared with certain German inline types of the late 1920s, though Augusta is considered an original design. Olocco tightened the letter spacing, regularised the grid construction across the full character set, and retained an alternate round E, accessible via OpenType features, that carries the Art Nouveau flavour present in a handful of the original letters.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe AM Augusta as an all-caps inline geometric typeface of complex construction, with each character featuring a solid side and a shaded side made up of thin parallel lines in a grid. They note it as a prime example of modernist type design with some Art Nouveau traits, reflecting how Art Nouveau letterforms remained fashionable in Italy into the late 1930s. The revival by Riccardo Olocco is faithful to the original metal type from the Neapolitan Fonderia De Luca, with improvements to consistency, spacing and certain figures.

name: Baxos
classification_notes: A square sans-serif with near-flat vertical curves and large x-height, rooted in geometric principles drawn from Suprematist form exploration. The absence of contrast and squarish construction place it firmly in the geometric grotesque tradition, though the indented stems introduce a highly individual character.
summary: A square geometric sans by Jennifer Lavia with signature indented stems, released as a variable font ranging from Light to Bold.
description: Designed by Jennifer Lavia from a Suprematist process workshop in Bari, the typeface builds its character around precisely placed indentations on vertical stems, each glyph carrying an alternate where the indent shifts position and shape. OpenType Contextual Alternates allow the two sets to interchange within a word or line, producing a restless, rhythmic texture that reads well at text sizes without surrendering its distinctiveness at display scale. The variable weight axis and broad Latin language support make it genuinely versatile despite the eccentricity of its core premise.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Baxos as a square sans-serif variable font exploring the idea of pure form, with a large x-height, indented stems and open terminals. They trace its origins to a Suprematist process course held at Scuola Open Source in Bari in 2018, noting that the indents, described as bites on the stems, create a new rhythm and can be alternated using Contextual Alternates. They present it as multipurpose, suitable for branding, display, magazines and contemporary events.

name: Bettoni
classification_notes: Bettoni follows the canonical features of Bodonian 'modern' type: vertical stress, high thick-thin contrast, hairline serifs and small apertures, placing it firmly in the didone subcategory.
summary: A Bodonian serif by Riccardo Olocco in three optical sizes, built for demanding publishing work across books, magazines and newspapers.
description: Designed by Riccardo Olocco and released between 2023 and 2024, Bettoni draws on Giambattista Bodoni's roman types without replicating any single source, instead distilling the vertical axis, high contrast and hairline serifs characteristic of the neoclassical style. Three optical sizes cover the full publishing range: Text widens the letterforms and reduces contrast for use from 6 to 14pt, while Display tightens and refines them for 24pt and above. The family runs to nine weights with oblique companions throughout, and includes small caps, old-style and lining figures, tabular and proportional sets, plus twenty border ornaments traced from original Bodoni and Bettoni fleurons.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Bettoni as an extensive Bodonian family designed by Riccardo Olocco, offering three optical sizes — Text, Subhead and Display — that make it particularly well suited to publishing contexts from books to newspapers, in print and on screen. The name references Niccolò Bettoni (1770–1842), the Italian printer who used Bodoni's types to produce editions of Italian classics, famously including the first edition of Ugo Foscolo's Dei sepolcri in 1808.

name: Bissaboa
classification_notes: A single-weight unicase display face with high contrast derived from ribbon-like, geometrically constructed stroke bending rather than traditional calligraphic means. Intended exclusively for titling and display use.
summary: A unicase display face by Alessandro Bombieri built on ribbon-like letterforms whose high thick-and-thin contrast comes from geometrically bent strokes rather than calligraphic tradition.
description: Designed by Alessandro Bombieri and released in 2020, Bissaboa takes its name from a Venetian mythological creature associated with zigzag and serpentine movement. Its letterforms follow a strict geometric construction, with contrast produced entirely by the bending of continuous ribbon-like strokes, evoking the Möbius strip, the Ouroboros and the double helix. Two character sets are provided via OpenType stylistic sets: outlined glyphs as default and filled glyphs as an alternative, allowing the two to be combined for multicolour titling work.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Bissaboa as a maverick unicase display face with fluid, ribbon-like letterforms. Named after a Venetian mythological creature evoking whirlwind and serpentine movement, the design draws on geometric construction and high contrast to suggest coiling symbols such as DNA's double helix and the Ouroboros. The face ships with outlined and filled character sets that can be layered for multicolour lettering, alongside OpenType alternates for selected letters.

name: Blueberry Pancakes
classification_notes: An ultra-thin, squarish display sans with near-geometric construction, single-weight only and explicitly conceived for headlines rather than text setting, placing it firmly in the display category.
summary: An ultra-thin, squarish display sans designed by Massimo Pitis and finished by Tipiblu, originally drawn from single-line strokes for a 2008 Kenya Hara poster.
description: Conceived by Massimo Pitis from a single-line lettering exercise and later completed with a lowercase by Andrea Amato of Tipiblu, the typeface carries the marks of its origins: terminals formed by pinnacles or short strokes parallel to the stroke axis rather than the baseline, wide squarish proportions, and a 45° tail on the lowercase a. It comes in one weight only, with OpenType alternates for g, w, Q and @, alongside lining and oldstyle numerals. Suited to book covers, magazine headlines and exhibition graphics where an austere, geometric thinness is the point.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Blueberry Pancakes as an ultra-thin display sans serif with squarish letterforms and wide proportions, conceived by Massimo Pitis and fine-tuned by Tipiblu. They trace its origins to a 2008 poster for a Kenya Hara talk, where Pitis set type using thin parallel lines, and note that the design reflects his longstanding interest in letterform skeletons and geometric construction.

name: Brasilica
classification_notes: A text serif with moderate contrast, strong serifs and wide proportions, drawing on influences including Skolar and Swift. The sharp terminals and abrupt connections place it closer to transitional or contemporary text serif territory than a purely humanist design.
summary: A text serif designed at the University of Reading to support bilingual Portuguese and Brazilian indigenous language publishing, with wide proportions and strong serifs suited to varied printing conditions.
description: Developed as a Masters project by Rafael Dietzsch, Brasilica draws on Swift, Skolar and Proforma for its approach to text performance at small sizes. Wide proportions, moderate contrast and sharp, abrupt terminals give it a sturdy, crisp texture distinct from more neutral text faces. The family spans Regular to Black with a single italic, and first appeared publicly in the visual identity for ATypI 2015 in São Paulo.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Brasilica as a contemporary text typeface originally conceived for bilingual publications in Portuguese and Brazilian indigenous languages, requiring characters absent from most fonts. Its wide proportions, moderate contrast and strong serifs suit different printing conditions, while sharp corners and marked terminals produce a sturdy, crisp texture. Designed by Rafael Dietzsch and produced between 2011 and 2015, it made its public debut in the ATypI 2015 conference identity in São Paulo.

name: Brevier
classification_notes: Apparently geometric in construction but with Renaissance humanist characteristics, including modulated strokes, generous x-height and open apertures — placing it firmly in the humanist sans tradition despite its compact, functional purpose.
summary: A compact humanist sans by Riccardo Olocco, engineered for legibility at extremely small sizes with deep ink traps and open junctures that read as distinctive design features at larger settings.
description: Designed specifically for small and very small type — packaging, pharmaceutical leaflets, instruction booklets — Brevier functions reliably down to 3 points under adverse printing conditions. Its apparently geometric forms carry Renaissance humanist proportions underneath: generous x-height, open apertures and slightly modulated strokes. Ink traps at internal angles and open junctures compensate for ink spread at small sizes, and these structural choices become striking formal features when the type is set large. Available in static and variable font formats.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Brevier as a compact sans for long texts at small or very small sizes, noting its usefulness for packaging, pharmaceutical leaflets and anything requiring legibility down to 3 points. The name references the old typefounders' term for 8-point type. They highlight that geometric letterforms conceal Renaissance characteristics, and that the ink traps and open junctures designed to offset ink spread at small sizes become strong original design features at larger sizes. The family has been in production since 2007 and has been used for captions and credits in magazines including TipoItalia3.

name: Burghi
classification_notes: A chubby, rounded variable display face with optical size variants; its exaggerated weight and smooth letterforms place it firmly in the fat face / rounded display category, designed for large-scale decorative use.
summary: A chubby variable display face by Fabrizio Falcone and Alex Bossi, inspired by the logo of Burghy, Italy's first American-style fast-food chain, and the paninari subculture of 1980s Milan.
description: Fat, rounded letterforms evoke the graphic language of early fast-food branding, with the design rooted in the consumer hedonism of Milan's paninari youth culture. As a variable font it offers two optical size variants: tight, near-microscopic counters for use above 40pt and slightly wider counters for smaller settings down to 20pt. The character set includes three fast-food emojis and a bagel counters stylistic set, giving it a playful range suited to joyful or tongue-in-cheek communication.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Burghi as a variable display font designed by Fabrizio Falcone and Alex Bossi, produced between 2022 and 2023. Inspiration came from the logo of Burghy, the first Italian US-style fast-food chain, and from the paninari subculture of early 1980s Milan, a youth movement defined by consumer lifestyles, fashionable appearances and fast food. The font's lumpy, fat-and-rounded style is intended to evoke the hedonism of those times, making it suited to fun and joyful communication.

name: Capraia
classification_notes: Capraia draws from both Caslon and Baskerville traditions, combining bracketed serifs, medium contrast and a big x-height in a manner consistent with transitional serif classification. Unconventional letterforms and slanted roman italics give it a contemporary inflection.
summary: A book typeface by Giulio Galli born from the collision of Caslon and Baskerville, with unconventional letterforms that read quietly in text and reveal themselves at display sizes.
description: Designed between 2018 and 2020, Capraia began as a tribute to Caslon's Great Primer before Baskerville took hold, producing a seven-weight family with matching italics and variable font counterparts. A large x-height, medium contrast and wide bracketed serifs anchor it firmly in continuous reading, while details like ball terminals, an idiosyncratic G and Q, and slanted roman italics keep it from feeling conventional. The Book weight addresses lighter preferences common among Italian publishers, and the slightly heavier Regular is pitched at the wider international market.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Capraia as a book typeface with a quirky character at large sizes and an irregular but attractive rhythm in text setting. They note that Book and Regular weights are designed specifically for continuous text, and that details including flattened curves, unconventional roman forms and slanted roman italics give the family a contemporary appeal alongside its publishing vocation.

name: Cemer
classification_notes: A geometric monospaced unicase display face derived from calligraphic experiments at 90° nib angle, producing a strict grid of thick horizontal and thin vertical strokes that gives it a distinct modular character beyond conventional sans categories.
summary: A geometric monospaced unicase face drawn from the 1960s logo of a Salentine ceramic tile company, with alternate letterforms built on a grid of thick horizontal and thin vertical strokes.
description: Derived from research into the visual identity of Cemer of Calimera, a short-lived southern Italian tile manufacturer, Michele Galluzzo's typeface translates a self-taught graphic designer's logo into a full unicase character set. Each letter exists in two versions, alternates toggling within the strict modular grid that also shaped the original tiles. Though the construction looks purely geometric, it originated in calligraphic experiments with a broad nib held at 90° to the baseline.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Cemer as a geometric monospaced unicase typeface whose shapes evoke the post-WW2 modernist aesthetic, drawing its name and letterforms from the logo of a ceramic tile manufacturer active in southern Italy in the 1960s and 1970s. They contextualise the release within designer Michele Galluzzo's ongoing historical research into Marcello D'Andrea and the Cemer company, framing the typeface as a way to shed light on an underrepresented chapter of Italian design history.

name: Chikoria
classification_notes: A display script with inverted thick/thin contrast drawn from flat-nib and pointed-brush calligraphic traditions, explicitly designed for titling and display use rather than continuous text.
summary: A single-weight calligraphic display script with inverted thick/thin contrast and hooked entry strokes, designed by Riccardo Riggio for CAST Studies in 2021.
description: Chikoria inverts the conventional flat-nib stress, placing thick strokes at the top and bottom of letterforms rather than left and right, producing a jaunty, energetic rhythm suited to titling. Lowercase letters are unjoined save for ligatures, and deep inktraps balance dark areas throughout. The cursive uppercase carries traditional proportions, while discretionary ligatures including ct, ft, st and tt extend its calligraphic range.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Chikoria as an unusual script face for display work, designed by Riccardo Riggio with a decisively quirky appearance suited to light-hearted entertainment and cheerful communication. The typeface features inverted thick/thin contrast, hooked entry strokes and tapered exits drawn from both flat-nib and pointed-brush calligraphic traditions, with deep inktraps added to balance colour across letters. The name references chicory in both Italian and Japanese, reflecting Riggio's intention to give the typeface an intercultural passport.

name: Dic Sans
classification_notes: The page explicitly describes it as a square sans serif. It draws on Novarese's Eurostile but is less geometric, with wider apertures, placing it in the square grotesque tradition rather than pure geometric.
summary: A square sans serif by Luciano Perondi, conceived in 2004 and developed over a decade, taking Eurostile as a starting point but opening up the apertures for better legibility at small sizes.
description: Conceived in conversation with Lucio Lazzara in 2004 and refined through visual identity projects over the following ten years, this square sans offers ten weights from Thin to ExtraBlack. Unlike Eurostile, the design pulls back from strict geometry in favour of wider apertures, which helps it hold up in captions and footnotes as well as at headline scale. OpenType features include small caps, oldstyle figures, contextual arrows, expressive punctuation sets and multiple stylistic alternates.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Dic Sans as a square sans serif suited to publishing and business communications, working well both on screen and in print. Inspired by Novarese's Eurostile but with wider apertures and a less geometric feel, it was conceived in 2004 during a conversation with Lucio Lazzara and tested and refined over four years through real visual identity projects. Its many weights make it useful for headlines and branding, and it performs particularly well at small sizes for notes and captions in books and magazines.

name: Divenire
classification_notes: Described by the foundry as a sanserif display face with squarish, irregular and discontinuous letterforms; its deliberate lack of uniformity and organic construction place it firmly in the display category rather than a conventional grotesque or geometric sans.
summary: An organic display sans by Luciano Perondi, originally created for the brand identity of the Partito Democratico Italiano, with squarish irregular letterforms and 28 distinct ampersands.
description: Designed in collaboration with FF3300 studio for an Italian political party, the face turns deliberate inconsistency into a coherent visual voice: horizontal terminations, angular junctures and alternating curved and straight strokes give it a lively rhythm that holds up in text settings as well as display. The family spans seven weights from Light to Black with matching italics, a variable font version, and 1264 glyphs including an expressive punctuation system with named marks such as Irony, Doubt and Love drawn as a nod to Hervé Bazin. OpenType features include multiple stylistic sets, oldstyle and tabular figures, and contextual alternates for directional arrows.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Divenire as a sanserif display face with an organic quality and a strong personality, noting that its squarish, irregular and discontinuous letterforms demonstrate how a lack of uniformity can produce consistent results. Originally designed with FF3300 for the Partito Democratico Italiano, it includes a complete set of expressive punctuation and 28 different ampersands across 7 styles with matching italics.

name: Divenire Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced sans-serif with humanist characteristics including slanted stroke terminations, varied axis and organic asymmetrical letterforms, placing it clearly in the humanist mono category.
summary: A humanist monospaced sans by Luciano Perondi that carries the organic letter structure of Divenire into fixed-width spacing for coding and typesetting.
description: Seven weights from Light to Black, each with a matching italic, plus variable font versions across the same range. Slanted stroke terminations on i, j, q, u and l, a shifting axis in g, o, d and c, and tendentially square letterforms all distinguish it from the more mechanical grid-bound mono. Extensive OpenType support includes contextual arrow ligatures, multiple stylistic sets, oldstyle figures and a full set of fractions.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Divenire Mono as a low-contrast monospaced sans with a humanistic imprint designed for programming, typesetting and printing. Despite its modular spacing, it retains the defining features of Divenire: low contrast, vertical strokes with slanted terminations, some variation of axis, and organic, asymmetrical, tendentially square letterforms.

name: Ebano
classification_notes: An extended grotesque sans whose letterforms are built from short straight segments mimicking woodcut and linocut carving, placing it firmly in the display category despite a grotesque structural foundation with two-storey 'a' and 'g'.
summary: An extended grotesque sans built from short straight segments to replicate the rough chisel-marks of hand-carved wood and lino type, drawing direct lineage from Koch's Neuland and Preissig's linocut lettering.
description: Designed by Stefano Barel during a course at CFP Bauer in Milan, Ebano uses polygonal approximations of curves rather than true arcs, so the 'O' reads more as a dodecagon than a circle at display sizes. The extended proportions recall sanserifs in the tradition of Frutiger's Univers, while the deliberate imperfections, slight tapering on straight strokes and irregular outlines, reproduce the physical action of a woodcutter's chisel. A single Regular weight makes it a focused display choice for titles, posters and logotypes rather than a workhorse text family.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Ebano as an extended spiky sanserif inspired by woodcuts and linocuts, with funky uneven lines, a generous x-height and confident strokes. They note it can manage long texts but fully reveals its personality at display sizes, and trace its heritage to Rudolf Koch's Neuland and Vojtěch Preissig's carved letterforms of the 1920s.

name: Ernst
classification_notes: Ernst is a slab serif drawing on early 20th-century avant-garde faces such as Tango and Belwe Antiqua. Its pronounced details and playful italic mark it as a display-oriented slab, though its large x-height and consistent proportions extend its range to text settings.
summary: A playful slab serif by Léon Hughes that blends details from Ernst Deutsch/Dryden's Tango and Georg Belwe's Belwe Antiqua into a seven-weight family with a distinctly theatrical italic.
description: Drawing on peculiar early 20th-century avant-garde typefaces rather than conventional slab traditions, Ernst has pronounced, idiosyncratic details that make it well suited to display work without abandoning text usefulness. Its large x-height and consistent proportions give it staying power across long reads, while the italic takes clear inspiration from Parisian street-theatre lettering of the silent-film era. Old-style figures, small caps and stylistic alternates add typographic depth across all 14 styles.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Ernst as an elegant but playful slab serif that harks back to peculiar early 20th-century types while responding to contemporary demands. Rather than a straight revival, it blends details from Ernst Deutsch/Dryden's Tango and Georg Belwe's Belwe Antiqua. The family runs from Thin to ExtraBold with matching italics, and includes old-style figures, small caps and alternate letters. The name honours Ernst Deutsch/Dryden, the Austrian designer behind Tango, with CAST noting that 'ernst' means 'serious' in German — while the typeface itself is anything but.

name: Estrojeune
classification_notes: An upright, non-connecting script with horizontal stroke contrast, designed as a display face with fluid, sinuous letterforms. The foundry explicitly describes it as a script face, distinct from its serif or sans-serif relatives.
summary: An upright display script by Valentina Casali with horizontal contrast and extensive OpenType contextual alternates, referencing Aldo Novarese's Estro of 1961.
description: Designed by Valentina Casali at CAST and developed between 2023 and 2025, the face takes its name and horizontal contrast from Novarese's Estro (Nebiolo, 1961) while adopting the fluid rhythm of a script. Narrow proportions and a generous x-height suit headlines, packaging and festival branding; positional OpenType features trigger automatic glyph substitutions within words, and swash capitals enable distinctive drop caps. The glyph set extends to arrows, astronomical and astrological symbols, and gender and sexual orientation marks.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Estrojeune as an upright script with horizontal contrast, intended for bold visual identities including music festivals, playful brands, packaging, titles and headings. Designer Valentina Casali frames it as a playful riposte to Novarese's Estro: where Estro was 'the typeface of a smile', Estrojeune is 'the typeface of a smirk'. The family includes swash capitals, contextual alternates, a range of symbols, and broad Latin Extended language support.

name: Fabbrica
classification_notes: Fabbrica uses squared curves, even proportions and technical-geometric letterforms consistent with the geometric sans-serif genre, with an industrial character distinguishing it from purely humanist or grotesque approaches.
summary: A geometric sans by Michele Patanè with an industrial character, offered in rounded and sharp-cornered variants across eight weights.
description: Designed by Michele Patanè and developed between 2019 and 2022, Fabbrica takes its name from the Italian word for factory and draws on industrial production as a formal reference. The family splits into two variants: Fabbrica with rounded corners for a warmer feel, and Fabbrica Sharp with hard corners suited to high-contrast headlines or compensating for optical rounding at small sizes. Sixteen styles in total, from Thin to ExtraBold with matching italics, alongside a generous set of OpenType alternates including constructed style, streamlined uppercases and circled figures.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Fabbrica as a distinctive sanserif inspired by industrial production, built for branding, publishing, advertising and technical publications. Its big x-height, even proportions and squared curves make it solid and versatile, while the two variants, rounded and sharp, give designers control over warmth and punch at different sizes and in different reproduction conditions.

name: Fabbrica Sharp
classification_notes: Squared curves, technical-geometric letterforms and even proportions place this firmly in the geometric sans genre; the sharp-cornered construction distinguishes it from its rounded sibling Fabbrica.
summary: A geometric sans with deliberately sharp corners, designed by Michele Patanè for branding, publishing and technical applications across eight weights.
description: Fabbrica Sharp is the hard-edged variant of Patanè's Fabbrica family, where squared curves and sharp corners resist the natural optical rounding that occurs at small sizes or on low-resolution devices. Eight weights from Thin to ExtraBold each carry an italic, and an extensive set of OpenType features includes constructed style, streamlined uppercase, circled figures, tailless l and open c variants. The sharp construction lends a direct, industrial character suited to headlines and display settings as much as to functional body copy.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Fabbrica Sharp as the angular counterpart to Fabbrica's rounded variant, noting that sharp corners counter excessive natural rounding at small sizes and low-resolution output, while delivering a punchier quality at larger sizes. The family of 16 styles spans eight weights with italics throughout, and is available in Desktop, Web and App formats.

name: AM Fiume
classification_notes: An all-caps geometric sans with a distinctive three-dimensional, half-tube construction derived from a 1934 Italian wood type original; its sculptural stroke treatment and decorative character place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A digital revival of a rare 1934 Italian wood type, characterised by half-tube strokes with cut grooves that create a three-dimensional highlight effect across an all-caps geometric alphabet.
description: The original wood type was produced by Xilografia Meneghello e Belluzzo in Legnago around 1934, and no comparable letterforms from other Italian or European makers appear to exist. Designer Michele Patanè describes the revival as extremely literal, preserving idiosyncrasies such as the jagged alignment caused by curved vertical terminals, while making careful adjustments to proportions and groove thickness for digital use. Two OpenType stylistic sets offer taller parentheses and brackets for legibility, alongside historically accurate alternates for the comma and ampersand.
foundry_blurb: CAST categorise AM Fiume under their Modernisti collection, describing it as a three-dimensional all-caps sans with geometric construction whose strokes resemble half-tubes with cut grooves forming highlights. The revival, designed by Michele Patanè and released in 2025, is based on a unique wood type produced by Xilografia Meneghello e Belluzzo in Verona around 1934, with courtesy access to the Luca Lattuga Archive and Tipoteca Italiana Fondazione.

name: Franco Stone
classification_notes: Franco Stone is a flared sans-serif with inscriptional roots in early Renaissance letterforms; its stems thicken toward terminals and curves approximate serifs without fully crossing into serif territory, placing it in the tradition of Optima rather than pure grotesque or humanist sans.
summary: A flared sans-serif by Lucas Franco rooted in early Renaissance Florentine inscriptions, sharing lineage with Optima but leaning toward a sharper, more lapidary elegance.
description: Drawing on the same 15th-century Florentine letterforms that prompted Hermann Zapf to sketch Optima in 1950, Franco Stone pursues a thinner, more incisive interpretation of the carved letter. Capitals with deliberately varied proportions, perfect circular O and Q forms, and a wide set of discretionary and nested ligatures drawn from Romanesque inscriptions give the face a strong, irregular classical rhythm. Lowercase letters reference Renaissance calligraphic practice, with an f bearing a descending stroke and a q with an uppercase-style tail.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Franco Stone as a flared sans-serif with early Renaissance inscriptional echoes, influenced by letter carving and the so-called 'Florentine letter' as discussed by Nicolete Gray and realised by stone carvers such as Michael Harvey. The foundry note its varied capital proportions, ligature set, and the way its stems swell toward terminals to approximate serifs, making it suited to select display applications requiring refinement and clarity.

name: Fulmar
classification_notes: Explicitly described as a modern Scotch, drawing on Alexander Wilson of Fife, 17th-century French modulation and 18th-century Belgian influence, placing it firmly in the Scotch Roman branch of the modern serif tradition.
summary: A modern Scotch serif by Leo Philp, drawing on Alexander Wilson, 17th-century French modulation and 18th-century Belgian type for extended reading.
description: Designed by Leo Philp and developed between 2016 and 2020, it synthesises Scottish, French and Belgian historical precedents into a contemporary structure with five weights and matching italics. Small caps, oldstyle and tabular figures, fractions, superscript, subscript and six stylistic sets give it the typographic range expected of serious book work. The foundry includes variable font versions across all ten styles, and the character set covers well over eighty Latin-script languages.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Fulmar as a modern Scotch intended for extended reading, more European than American in character, drawing on influences from around the North Sea including Alexander Wilson of Fife, 17th-century French experiments in modulation and 18th-century Belgian flash. The result is described as crisp yet warm, rational but humane, suited to new translations, new histories and new understanding. Five weights, ten styles, small caps and a wide set of OpenType features are included, alongside what the foundry call unicorn manicules.

name: AM Futurismo
classification_notes: A counterless all-caps geometric sans built from circles, triangles and rectangles with slight rounding and unexpected thick/thin contrast; its single weight, all-caps nature and wood-type revival origin place it firmly in display rather than text use.
summary: A digital revival of a circa-1930 Italian wood type, built from circles, triangles and rectangles with slightly rounded edges and deliberately retained idiosyncrasies.
description: Originally produced by Xilografia Ruggero Zuliani of Verona, the source material was coarse and inconsistent; Bombieri has regularised the terminals and refined numerals while preserving the organic irregularity of characters like C, G and Q. The counterless all-caps construction sits on basic geometric primitives, but unexpected thick/thin contrast on strokes such as the stem of P gives it a character absent from more clinical geometric revivals. Well suited to headlines, posters and any context where Italian Futurist graphic energy is appropriate.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe AM Futurismo as a counterless all-caps sans with a simple construction based on circles, triangles and rectangles, with slightly rounded corners and a peculiar thick/thin contrast appearing even in unexpected places like the stem of P. The original was released around 1930 by Xilografia Ruggero Zuliani of Verona. In the digital revival, Bombieri equalised all edges and terminals for uniformity while keeping certain idiosyncrasies — such as the organic shapes of C, G and Q — close to their original forms.

name: Gil Modern
classification_notes: A low-contrast display typeface with Lombardic capitals and rounded seriffed lowercase, rooted in medieval and Catalan Art Nouveau lettering traditions rather than any conventional text serif classification.
summary: A single-weight display typeface by Valerio Monopoli drawing on Lombardic capitals and Catalan Art Nouveau lettering, named after the patron of Barcelona's Hospital de la Santa Creu i de Sant Pau.
description: Designed around the ornamental P and G letterforms found throughout Lluís Domènech i Montaner's Hospital de la Santa Creu i de Sant Pau, the typeface extends those Lombardic capitals across a full alphabet, pairing them with a rounded seriffed lowercase informed by Domènech's own modernist magazine mastheads. Swashes and drop terminals appear in both cases, and several stylistic sets offer more conventional alternates where the more extreme forms would be out of place. Suited to dropcaps, display headlines and decorative titling, it carries a medieval weight alongside the organic curves of fin-de-siècle Catalan design.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Gil Modern as a distinctive low-contrast display typeface with Lombardic capitals and rounded lowercase letters, also suitable for small-size typesetting. Named after Pau Gil i Serra, patron of the Hospital de la Santa Creu i de Sant Pau, the typeface was developed by Valerio Monopoli through an extensive analysis of the symbolic and cultural meaning behind Domènech i Montaner's architectural and typographic work, drawing on Catalan romanesque style, Art Nouveau ornament and the architect's own lettering for publication mastheads.

name: Gramma
classification_notes: A compact, large x-height sans with squarish arches and distinctive bird-beak terminals that recall calligraphic construction, placing it in the humanist sans tradition despite its tight, compressed proportions.
summary: A compact humanist sans by Riccardo Olocco with a large x-height and distinctive bird-beak terminals, designed for both headline and text use across print and screen.
description: Developed from 2009 through 2022, Gramma pairs a high x-height and tight proportions with squarish arches whose counters push outward, creating tension in the compressed letterforms. Its most individual feature is the bird-beak terminal, used consistently across c, e, f and s, which echoes the unconnected junctures found in a, b, d and g. Five weights from Light to Bold each have matching italics, and the family is available as a variable font with broad Latin language support.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Gramma as a compact sans with a big x-height suited to both headlines and body text for print and screen. Its design emerged from an exploration of how letters read at extreme sizes, with squarish arches and outward-pushing counters balancing the compressed forms. The typeface's defining characteristic is its bird-beak terminals, which mirror the unconnected junctures between stem and curve visible in letters such as a, b, d and g. Completed with matching italics in 2022, Gramma has been used in printed projects ranging from branding to magazines since its early development.

name: Iki Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced typeface with a fixed-width system across all widths and weights; the sans serif structure with slight contrast and letterform details drawn from 19th-century grotesques places it firmly in the grotesque mono subcategory.
summary: A monospaced grotesque by Beatrice D'Agostino spanning five widths from Compressed to Extra Expanded, designed equally for publishing and coding environments.
description: Built across six weights and five widths with slanted counterparts for each, the family runs to over forty styles plus variable font versions. Letterforms draw on 19th-century grotesque details and carry a degree of contrast unusual in monospaced type, while the non-slanted cuts apply a subtle leftward inclination to the letters. The name comes from the Japanese word for breath, and the design handles the constraints of fixed-width spacing with evident care rather than compromise.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Iki Mono as a multifaceted monospaced typeface designed for both publishing and coding. Its sans serif structure references 19th-century grotesques in certain letterforms and degree of contrast, while non-oblique versions feature letters very slightly slanted leftwards, recalling the character of typewriter faces. The family spans forty weights and styles from Compressed Thin to Extra Expanded Bold, including slanted versions throughout.

name: AM Impero
classification_notes: A geometrically constructed sans-serif with extreme thick/thin contrast and a consistent 9-degree incline throughout, placing it firmly in the display category as a revival of a pre-war Italian wood type modernist face.
summary: A revival of a pre-war Italian wood type by Xilografia di Verona, redrawn and optically corrected by Antonella Sartorelli with a stark 9-degree incline and extreme thick/thin contrast.
description: Based on a wood type original released by Xilografia di Verona before World War II, this single-weight geometric sans carries a 9-degree angle throughout and restricts each character to a single thick stroke, except M. Refinements for this revival include standardised thick/thin modulation, redrawn numerals and ampersand, and three OpenType stylistic sets offering normal, long and looped ascenders and descenders. The looped variant bears a close resemblance to the early Agfa logo from around 1925, suggesting a possible common source.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe AM Impero as a slightly inclined geometrical sans-serif with very strong thick/thin contrast, a 9-degree angle and several unusual features, including a G with a thin curve on the left, a narrow s and S, and curved strokes in M, N, V, W, v and w. Designer Antonella Sartorelli aimed to preserve the original character of the typeface while improving its visual balance, standardising modulation and redrawing inconsistent glyphs. Following the wood type model, the font includes three alternative sets of ascenders and descenders: normal, long and long-with-loops.

name: Ipazia
classification_notes: Rooted in late 19th- and early 20th-century Elzevir typefaces, Ipazia combines bracketed serifs with unusual weight progression, where the ExtraBold approaches a contrasted sans, placing it in a transitional yet unconventional serif category.
summary: A text serif by Luciano Perondi drawing on late 19th- and early 20th-century Elzevir typefaces, with deliberate irregularity built into its proportions and weight progression.
description: Designed by Luciano Perondi and rooted in Italian and German newspaper types of the Elzevir tradition, Ipazia intentionally courts inconsistency: the regular weight is fully seriffed, while the ExtraBold sheds its serifs to become a contrasted sans. That progression is not a technical oversight but a design position, pushing formal variation across weights to animate the text without disrupting overall page colour. The family currently covers the Text optical size in five weights with matching italics, with display sizes in development.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Ipazia as a typeface family with an optical size for text, joined soon by display sizes, featuring five roman and italic weights from Book to ExtraBold. Inspired by late 19th- and early 20th-century Elzevir typefaces known for editorial use and a certain formal naivety, Perondi pushed unconventional proportions and striking irregularity in white space distribution to their limits, while keeping the overall visual effect of the page intact. The ExtraBold weight features diminutive serifs that bring it close to a contrasted sans serif, an approach also applied to the italic, which draws on an early 20th-century roman from the Dresden foundry later known as Schriftguss AG.

name: Jantar Flow
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a humanist sanserif; low contrast and large x-height are hallmarks of humanist sans design aimed at continuous reading.
summary: A humanist sans by Radek Łukasiewicz built for continuous reading across print and screen, with broad European language coverage and a thorough OpenType feature set.
description: Seven weights from ExtraLight to Heavy, each with a proper italic, give the family range for both body text and display use. A large x-height and low contrast help it hold at small sizes in captions and side notes, while tabular and proportional figures in both lining and oldstyle styles, plus a hybrid figure set, cover most editorial and typographic needs. Extensive diacritics cover all European languages, and case-sensitive characters add polish for all-caps settings.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Jantar Flow as a humanist sanserif tailored for continuous reading in print and on screen. Its large x-height and low contrast make it effective at small sizes, and it pairs with a sister family, Jantar Sharp. The family offers fourteen styles, a full set of figure variants, superscripts, subscripts, and extensive diacritics covering all European languages.

name: Jantar Sharp
classification_notes: Jantar Sharp occupies an intentional middle ground between serif and sans, defined by flared stroke terminals rather than bracketed serifs; it draws from multiple historical periods without committing to a single model, placing it closest to a flared or transitional hybrid.
summary: A text family by Radek Łukasiewicz built on flared terminals that sits between serif and sans, optimised for continuous reading across print and screen.
description: Seven weights from ExtraLight to Heavy, each with a matching italic, give the family considerable range without straying from its core purpose of sustained reading. The flared stroke endings borrow selectively from different historical periods rather than replicating any single model, producing shapes that feel organic rather than stylistically prescribed. OpenType features include proportional and tabular figures in both oldstyle and lining variants, small capitals, superscripts, subscripts and extensive diacritics covering all European languages.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Jantar Sharp as a text family whose flared terminals put it outside the conventional serif/sans distinction, combining the most recognisable features of both to achieve high legibility standards. The italic offers contrast without disrupting the pace of longer passages, and the family ships with a thorough set of OpenType features including hybrid figures sitting between x-height and capitals.

name: Kornel
classification_notes: Inspired by Renaissance typography with classic proportions, sharp terminals and diagonal stroke treatment; the gently slanted italics and simplified letterforms place it firmly in the humanist old-style tradition.
summary: A Renaissance-inspired text serif by Radek Łukasiewicz, built across seven weights with matching italics for demanding long-form composition.
description: Drawn with classic Renaissance proportions as its reference point rather than any single typeface, Kornel uses sharp entry and exit strokes and distinctive diagonal treatments to give letters such as R, K, k, v, x and y a simplified, contemporary character. The seven-weight range runs from Book to Black, each with a corresponding italic whose oblique strokes keep the companion light and readable. Variable font files are included alongside static instances, covering an extensive range of Latin-script languages.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Kornel as a seriffed family designed for long texts, with its confident appearance derived from classic proportions and sharp details suited to print and web. Inspiration came from Renaissance typography broadly rather than any specific typeface, with terminal and diagonal stroke treatment providing a contemporary twist, while the gently slanted italics complement the roman through simplified forms in letters such as R, K, k, v, x, y and w.

name: AM Liguria
classification_notes: A digital revival of a 1938 Italian wood type, the face is monolinear, slanted throughout and evocative of German geometric sans-serifs of the 1920s. Its exclusively italic posture and wood-type origins place it firmly in the display category despite its sans-serif structure.
summary: A digital revival of a 1938 Italian wood type, cut by the Xilografia Verona and faithfully redrawn by Beatrice D'Agostino with refined counters and consistent terminals.
description: Originally available in five sizes as a wood type, Liguria is a slanted monolinear sans with classical capital proportions, narrow B, E and S alongside a wide O. Its defining feature is the flat base and top of diagonal letters such as A, V and W, a practical device that reduces the need for kerning. D'Agostino's revival adjusts proportions and contrast to preserve the original's rough, spontaneous quality while reworking the punctuation entirely.
foundry_blurb: CAST describes AM Liguria as a slanted monolinear sans with a raw, utilitarian appearance vaguely reminiscent of German geometric sanserifs of the 1920s. The face is a faithful digital revival of a wood type cut by the Xilografia Verona around 1938, with designer Beatrice D'Agostino refining counters, harmonising terminals and completely redesigning the punctuation to retain the original's roughness and spontaneity.

name: AM Lombardia
classification_notes: An all-caps condensed sans-serif with high thick/thin contrast and an 8-degree slant, characteristics that push it firmly into display territory rather than general-purpose sans-serif use.
summary: A digital revival of a 1941 Florentine metal type, redrawn by Luciano Perondi and Carlotta Albiero with added lowercase and alternates.
description: Originally patented by Pierallini, Turchi & Co. in Florence in 1941 and designed by graphic designer Egidio Bozzolo, Lombardia's all-caps condensed forms carry a strong 8-degree slant, high thick/thin contrast and a characteristic small curve at the central intersections of B, K and R. The digital revival closely follows the original metal type in its uppercase, figures and punctuation, while Perondi and Albiero extended it with lowercase letters modelled partly on the 1954 Spanish typeface Ilerda, plus alternate characters including the E and M variants present in Bozzolo's original. The result sits within CAST's Modernisti series, which collects revivals of Italian modernist-era type.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe AM Lombardia as a revival of a metal type patented in Florence in 1941, designed originally by Egidio Bozzolo and featuring geometric letterforms with high contrast, an 8-degree slant and distinctive rounded intersections recalling Nebiolo's Fluidum. The digital version was drawn by Carlotta Albiero and Luciano Perondi, who added lowercase letters following the approach of the 1954 Spanish face Ilerda, and included alternate glyphs for a, k, t, E and M.

name: AM Macallè
classification_notes: A bold, compressed sanserif revival of a 1936 Italian wood type, featuring Art Déco details such as low crossbars that extend beyond stems and wide circular alternates; classified as display given its single weight, compressed proportions and decorative lineage.
summary: A digital revival of a bold Italian wood type from 1936, faithful to Xilografia Internazionale's Macallè with an inline variant and wide circular alternates accessed via OpenType stylistic sets.
description: Originally issued as wood type by Xilografia Internazionale of Legnago around 1936 and available up to 20-line sizes, the face is notable for its compressed proportions, very large x-height and Art Déco low crossbars that extend leftward beyond the stems. Designer Alessandro Colizzi's revival incorporates the former inline companion Adigrat as a stylistic set alongside a second set of wide, circular alternates for C, G and O, making the whole system more flexible than its historical source. The result suits poster and headline work where early-twentieth-century Italian graphic vernacular is the reference point.
foundry_blurb: CAST place AM Macallè in their Modernisti collection, describing it as remarkably faithful to the original wood type. They note that the digital revival allowed optimisation of design and spacing, and that OpenType features give access to wide circular alternatives and the inline version formerly known as Adigrat, with matching details and proportions ensuring flawless interchangeability between styles within text settings.

name: Macchia
classification_notes: A counterless display sans rooted in Italian 1930s lettering traditions, with blotted counters and high-contrast dark forms that preclude running text use; squarely a display category face.
summary: A counterless display sans by Ruggero Magrì rooted in Italian 1930s lettering, with blotted forms and a broad-nib calligraphic energy built for loud headlines and book jackets.
description: Emerging from Magrì's experiments in broad-nib calligraphy, Macchia updates the Italian counterless sans tradition of the 1930s with a new lowercase set that the originals never had. Tight fitting and a large x-height make it compact at headline sizes, while tapering apertures and rounded terminals pull the design away from rigid modernist monoliths toward something more playful. Available in a single Black weight, it is unashamedly a face for shouting.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Macchia as a funky upgrade of Italian counterless sans from the 1930s, a style associated with art déco and modernism that is now seeing a digital revival. Designer Ruggero Magrì combines the forthrightness of counterless letterforms with a comic's touch, adding a new lowercase alongside the capitals and shaping the result as a display face for publishing and branding, conceived to kidnap whitespace and ransom it for attention.

name: Macho Modular
classification_notes: A sans-serif with a geometric, systematic character derived from its mathematically related modular width system, where each letter maintains the same width across all weights, drawing inspiration from Olivetti Margherita and Linotype machine systems.
summary: A sans with a mathematically defined modular width system, originally designed for the MAN museum in Nuoro as part of its corporate identity.
description: Designed by Luciano Perondi in 2010 for the Museo d'Arte Provincia di Nuoro, the typeface uses a limited set of letter widths related to one another by fixed mathematical ratios. Every letter retains the same width regardless of weight, enabling vertical alignment across weights without glyph distortion, much as a monospaced font would. The system was inspired by the Olivetti Margherita and Linotype machine traditions, and the family spans five weights with matching italics plus variable font versions.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Macho Modular as a sans with modular widths, designed in 2010 for the MAN (Museo d'Arte Provincia di Nuoro) as part of a corporate identity by Sabina Era, covering logo, textual communication and signage. Its limited letter widths are mathematically interrelated, and every letter holds the same width across all weights, permitting vertical alignment without glyph distortion. The system was inspired by the Olivetti Margherita system and Linotype machines.

name: Macho Moustache
classification_notes: Shares modular construction with its sibling Macho Modular but adopts the Grotesque tradition of tight apertures on a, c, e and s, placing it firmly in the grotesque sans-serif category.
summary: A grotesque companion to Macho Modular by Luciano Perondi, distinguished by tight apertures on key letters and designed around Stefan Themerson's concept of semantic typography.
description: Where Macho Modular takes an open approach, Macho Moustache pulls in the apertures on a, c, e and s to follow the tighter conventions of the grotesque tradition, while retaining the shared modular widths and letterforms of the Macho family. Five weights from Thin to Bold are offered in upright and slanted cuts, along with variable font versions of each. The family carries an extensive set of OpenType features including multiple stylistic sets, full figure variants and modular brackets and underlinings conceived for semantic typographic use.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Macho Moustache as closely related to Macho Modular, sharing its modular widths and most letterforms but following the Grotesque tradition of tight apertures for a, c, e, s and some numerals. Original design work began alongside Macho Modular in 2008, with the range expanded to five weights by the 2017 release. The family was designed with Themerson's semantic typography in mind, and accordingly includes full sets of modular brackets and underlinings.

name: Magmo
classification_notes: Magmo draws on Caslon's proportions from the 1727 type specimen while introducing highly irregular, organic stroke swellings derived from burning paper experiments, placing it in a contemporary expressive serif tradition.
summary: A contemporary serif by Nacir Imache whose stroke swellings are derived from burning paper experiments, set against proportions drawn from Caslon's 1727 type specimen.
description: The organic globules that swell within Magmo's strokes were generated by studying the charred edges of burning paper, producing a thick/thin contrast that intensifies markedly as weight increases. Despite this unusual formal origin, the design anchors itself in William Caslon's English and Great Primer body sizes, giving the lighter weights sufficient classical regularity for text use at small sizes. Nine weights with matching slanted versions are available as both static fonts and a variable font with weight and slant axes.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Magmo as a contemporary serif with fluid and unusually irregular shapes, suited to expressive headlines in its bolder weights and to text in its lighter ones. The organic forms originate in experiments with burning paper, producing swollen globules in the strokes that grow with weight. Proportions and details are based on William Caslon's original type, specifically the English and Great Primer sizes from his 1727 specimen. The family includes lining and old-style figures, fractions and a full variable font.

name: AM Majella
classification_notes: A joined-up script with strictly geometric construction based on circular proportions and no thick-thin contrast, derived from an Italian wood type original. The rationalist geometry and connecting strokes place it firmly in the geometric script category.
summary: A digital revival of a 1930s Italian wood type, fusing joined-up script with strict geometric construction derived from circular proportions.
description: Originally released by Xilografia di Verona between 1937 and 1939, the source wood type combined handwriting forms with rationalist geometry at almost 45-degree entry and exit strokes. Valentina Casali's revival preserves the original lowercase, adds a new condensed uppercase derived from the original figures, and uses OpenType contextual logic to smooth the letter connections that were imperfect in the historical source. A Circular O alternate is offered via stylistic sets.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe AM Majella as a lowercase-only, joined-up script with a strong geometric design, big x-height and no thick-thin contrast — an 'outrageous' fusion of handwriting and geometrical sans. The revival stays true to the original lowercase, extends it with new capitals based on the original figures, and uses OpenType features to handle initial, medial, final and isolated letter forms for smoother composition.

name: AM Marche
classification_notes: A digital revival of an Italian Art Deco wood type with modular geometric construction, idiosyncratic uppercase width contrasts and decorative horizontal stroke extensions; best suited to display use rather than continuous text.
summary: A single-weight digital revival of an Italian Art Deco wood type from the late 1930s, retaining the original's modular geometry, varying uppercase widths and the characteristic slanted 's' while refining its rough edges.
description: Drawn by Fabrizio Falcone from surviving type cases and the Xilografia di Verona catalogue, AM Marche preserves the geometric rigour and eccentric proportions of the source material: wide circular O, C, G and Q set against narrow companions, horizontal strokes extending beyond stems, and the 'escalator S' noted by historian Paul Shaw. Minor optical corrections have been applied throughout, including overshooting, subtle stroke modulation and more sinuous curves on B, D, P and R. The result sits in the CAST Modernisti collection alongside AM Tripoli, AM Novecento and AM Augusta, all concerned with Italian twentieth-century letterpress heritage.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe AM Marche as a geometric sans with Art Deco origins, revived from a wood-type design available in the Xilografia di Verona catalogue between 1937 and 1940. The digital version sticks closely to the original while polishing its roughness: horizontal strokes have been made shorter and more uniform, optical tweaks applied throughout, and the curves of B, D, P and R made more sinuous to work alongside the geometric rigour of the remaining letters.

name: MGF Sans
classification_notes: The designers describe it explicitly as a geometric grotesque, combining a clear geometric structure with grotesque conventions, citing Swiss Style influences and historical Milanese typefaces such as Forma and Helvetica.
summary: A geometric grotesque commissioned for the 2022 Milano Graphic Festival, drawing on Swiss Style rigour and the Milanese design tradition of Novarese's Forma.
description: Designed by Fabrizio Falcone as the custom face for Milano Graphic Festival, MGF Sans pairs the tight spacing and wide, uniform capitals of Swiss typography with geometric alternates for a, i, l, t and squared curves on f and j. The family was extended by Falcone and type developer Alex Bossi for the successor festival BIG, adding a Heavy weight, slanted italics and a variable font. A set of 16 directional arrow glyphs, referencing Vignelli and Noorda's New York subway signage, adds a quietly functional flourish.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe MGF Sans as a contemporary geometric grotesque born as the custom face of the 2022 Milano Graphic Festival, evoking the golden age of Milanese graphic design and the international reach of the Swiss Style. Designed by Fabrizio Falcone with input from festival director Francesco Dondina, and later developed with Alex Bossi, the family references Helvetica and Nebiolo's Forma as its historical anchors. It is available in Regular and Heavy weights with slanted italics and as a variable font.

name: Neon NBL
classification_notes: A monocase geometric sans-serif with modular construction, based on the 1936 Nebiolo typeface by Giulio Da Milano. The geometric structure and single-case design place it firmly in the geometric sans tradition.
summary: A faithful digital revival of Giulio Da Milano's 1936 Nebiolo typeface, expanded by Alessandro Colizzi into a 42-style family spanning seven widths and five weights, with a shaded Ombra variant.
description: Originally issued by Nebiolo in varying widths tied to specific type sizes, the design has been rationalised into a coordinated system running from UltraCondensed to Wide, each width offered in Thin through Bold plus the decorative Ombra shade. The monocase geometric construction is tempered by optical corrections at stem junctions and terminals, making it usable at text sizes as well as for headlines and signage. In 2022 it was selected for the Moët Hennessy office building signage in Paris by Barbarito Bancel and +fortuna.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Neon NBL as a faithful digital redesign of the Giulio Da Milano typeface released by Nebiolo in 1936, expanded by Alessandro Colizzi from its original size-specific width variants into a coordinated 42-style family. The updated release adds Ombra across all widths, over 700 glyphs per font with hand kerning, and an extended character set including superior and inferior sets, fractions, tabular figures and discretionary ligatures.

name: New Cordial
classification_notes: A geometric display face with organic, sinuous detail rooted in Italian Art Nouveau ('Stile Liberty') and early Modernist influences, placing it firmly in the display category rather than a structural text classification.
summary: A redesign and expansion of Riccardo Olocco's 2008 all-caps display face Cordial, now with a full lowercase, five weights and an inline bold, informed by the convergence of Italian Stile Liberty and early European Modernism.
description: Originally designed for Veer and unavailable since 2012, the typeface has been substantially reworked: the capitals are refined, a complete lowercase introduced and diacritic coverage extended across European languages. Distinctive details include a curved stem on A, egg-shaped round letters with a low centre of gravity, a narrow S and slightly slanted second stems on h, n, a and m. Alternate forms for a, g, u and y offer more conventional or more characterful options, and an inline Bold variant adds a decorative register suited to poster and titling work.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe New Cordial as combining sinuous lines and organic details within a geometric framework, evoking a moment in Italian typography when Art Nouveau letterforms blended with emerging Modernist ideas. Rather than reviving a specific historical artefact, it is a contemporary design informed by historical research, transforming those influences into a typeface that feels unexpectedly current.

name: AM Novecento
classification_notes: An all-caps geometric sans of Italian 1930s origin, constructed from squares and circles with pronounced thick/thin contrast in certain letters; its display-only, woodtype heritage and idiosyncratic letterforms place it firmly in the display category rather than general-purpose sans-serif.
summary: A digital revival of a 1937 Italian wood type, retaining its all-caps geometric construction and idiosyncratic details including the slanted 'escalator S' and gondola-bow A.
description: Originally produced by Xilografia di Verona between 1937 and 1940, this all-caps sans is built from squares and circles of uniform size, yielding a consistently rounded texture despite its dark weight. Certain letters carry strong thick/thin contrast, while others take unexpected forms: H is modelled on a lowercase h, and the sharply slanted S follows a fashionable 1930s convention noted by type historian Paul Shaw. The revival includes Venezia, the decorative variant with two horizontal white lines cutting through each letter, accessible as an OpenType stylistic set.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe AM Novecento as a dark geometric all-caps sans of elementary construction, with a striking rounded character arising from the arcs and circles that form its letters. The design is based on a wood type produced by Xilografia di Verona from 1937 to 1940, and the digital revival by Alessandro Bombieri applies optical corrections to improve visual balance in the digital environment while preserving the original modernist character. The decorative Venezia variant, featuring two white horizontal lines entering the letters from the left, is included as an OpenType stylistic set.

name: AM Panama
classification_notes: A single-weight geometric sans revival drawn from 1930s–40s Art Deco wood type, with idiosyncratic expressive details that push it firmly into display territory rather than workhorse sans-serif.
summary: A digital revival of a 1930s Italian wood type, bringing its Art Deco geometry and idiosyncratic detailing into a single-weight display face.
description: Drawn by Giulio Galli from wood type specimens held at the Tipoteca Italiana Fondazione, AM Panama restores a monolinear sanserif originally cut by the Xilografia Meneghello e Belluzzo in six body sizes during the 1930s and 40s. Its geometry is sharpened by pointed apexes, diamond-shaped stroke endings, exaggerated apertures and a missing crossbar on the A, giving it a loud, distinctive rhythm at display sizes. The original companion design Suez, a semi-outlined version of the same letterforms, is included as a stylistic set via OpenType.
foundry_blurb: CAST describes AM Panama as a dark sanserif with a big x-height, compact letterforms and near-monolinear contrast, punctuated by expressive details including diamond terminals, exaggerated apertures and a missing horizontal bar on the A. The digital revival by Giulio Galli refines the original proportions for screen use while preserving the Art Deco character of the wood type source, and bundles the outlined Suez variant as an OpenType stylistic set.

name: Pritzious
classification_notes: An unjoined formal script rooted in 18th-century Caractère de Finance types by Rosart and Fleischman, with high contrast and calligraphic capitals derived from Barbedor's copper-plate tradition.
summary: A high-contrast unjoined script by Valentina Casali, rooted in the 18th-century Caractère de Finance tradition of Rosart and Fleischman.
description: Drawing on two specific historical sources, Fleischman's 1756 Dubbelde Descendiaan Geschreven Schrift and Rosart's c.1760 Double Descendiaan, Casali synthesised a style rather than reviving a single typeface. Lowercase letters remain deliberately unjoined, turning a metal-type limitation into a defining characteristic, while the capitals carry Barbedor's signature thin-stemmed duty line also adopted by Bodoni. OpenType contextual alternates, historical forms and swash variants allow the typeface to behave dynamically in branding, packaging and display settings.
