foundry_blurb: CAST describe Pritzious as a flamboyant script typeface for branding and display, based on the Financière style of Rosart and Fleischman, with cultured shapes, European roots and a contemporary demeanour that sets it apart among scripts of its kind.

name: AM Radio
classification_notes: An all-caps, monolinear, modular sans with distinctive inline vertical stems and diagonally cropped terminals, reviving a 1930s Italian metal type. Its decorative inline feature and display-only character place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A digital revival of a 1930s Neapolitan metal typeface, distinguished by its inline vertical stems and diagonally cropped horizontal terminals.
description: Originally cast by Fonderia Tipografica Meridionale Armando De Luca in Naples around 1935, Radio is an all-caps monolinear sans with a modular construction. Its most striking feature is an inline that bisects vertical stems, with a solid stylistic set provided for small sizes where the inline becomes illegible. Designer Maria Jarzyna corrected inconsistencies in the original round forms, unified spacing, and redrew problematic characters including the ampersand and brackets while preserving the type's original rhythm and texture.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe AM Radio as part of their Alfabeti Modernisti collection, a digital revival of a modular all-caps sans originally produced in Naples around 1935. The typeface features characteristic diagonally cropped terminals and an unusual inline that splits vertical stems in two, with a solid version included as a stylistic set for use at smaller sizes or in combination with the inline variant.

name: Rhau
classification_notes: A digitisation of 16th-century woodcut titling letters from a 1539 Lutheran publication, with spiky serifs, extreme slanted strokes and a rough expressivity that places it firmly in the display category as a Renaissance revival.
summary: A digital revival of woodcut titling letters from Georg Rhau's 1539 Wittenberg publication, reconstructed into a complete typeface by Giulio Galli.
description: The original four letters — DISCANTUS, ALTUS, TENOR, BASSUS — were drawn from opening pages of the Officia Paschalia, a collection of Lutheran masses printed during the Reformation. Galli extended these peculiar caps, with their spiky serifs and extreme rightward slants, into a full alphabet with lowercase, numerals and punctuation, keeping the rough expressivity of the source material. The result sits somewhere between Renaissance roman and late 19th-century gothic revival, and is suited to display and titling work where texture and historical register matter.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Rhau as a type revival based on four woodcut letters from the title pages of the Officia Paschalia (1539), printed in Wittenberg by Georg Rhau, an important figure in the Protestant Reformation. Designer Giulio Galli built the typeface for the Early Notation Typesetter project developed by Versilian Studios, completing the full character set from scratch to complement the surviving woodcut capitals, which feature unusually dark strokes for Renaissance romans and distinctive spiky, curly serifs.

name: Rialto dF
classification_notes: Letterforms drawn directly from humanistic calligraphic tradition, with a roman and italic relationship modelled on Francesco Griffo's italic for Aldus Manutius. The calligraphic origins and Renaissance Venetian references place it firmly in the humanist serif category.
summary: A book typeface rooted in humanistic calligraphy, drawn by Giovanni De Faccio and Lui Karner from 1995 and offered in two optical sizes for different point-size ranges.
description: Born from a collaboration between the Austrian Lui Karner and Venetian Giovanni De Faccio, the letterforms were drawn directly in digital format from humanistic models carried in the designers' hands and memory. The roman and italic relationship follows the convention established by Francesco Griffo for Aldus Manutius's 1501 Virgil octavo, with uppercase, numerals and punctuation used alongside the italic. Two optical subfamilies -- Piccolo for sizes up to 14pt and Grande for 16pt and above -- are supported by an extensive set of OpenType features developed by Karsten Luecke, including small capitals, oldstyle figures, swash characters, discretionary ligatures and multiple stylistic sets.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Rialto dF as a book face inspired by calligraphic tradition, named after the famous Venetian bridge to evoke a crossing between calligraphy and typography, roman and italic, and between Italy and Austria. The letterforms were drawn from humanistic forms memorised by the designers, following the tradition of pairing upright uppercase with italic text established by Griffo for the Aldus Manutius Virgil of 1501. It comes in Piccolo (up to 14pt) and Grande (16pt and above), with alternate characters and dingbats via OpenType features developed by Karsten Luecke. Production ran from 1995 to 2016.

name: Roma Neue
classification_notes: A neutral, upright grotesque in the Swiss tradition, drawing on 19th-century jobbing types and 20th-century descendants such as Helvetica and Univers. Slanted rather than true italic styles confirm its neo-grotesque lineage.
summary: A Swiss-tradition grotesque by Stefano Cremisini, spanning eight weights with slanted italics and a variable font that allows continuous control over slant angle.
description: Designed between 2021 and 2023, it sits squarely in the neo-grotesque lineage of Helvetica and Univers, offering clean, upright forms suited to exhibition design, corporate identity, packaging and wayfinding. The variable font adds a slant axis running from 0° to 16°, while OpenType features include alternate forms for a, g, l, t, G, J and R, old-style figures, tabular figures and a full set of fractions. Over 560 glyphs cover an extensive range of Latin-script languages.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Roma Neue as a versatile and functional grotesque following the best examples of 19th-century jobbing types and their 20th-century descendants, including Helvetica and Univers. They position it as carrying the best visual features of the Swiss Style and suitable for exhibition design, corporate identity, artbooks, packaging, signage, wayfinding and UI and web design.

name: Rotunda Veneta
classification_notes: Rotunda Veneta is a digitisation of a 15th-century Southern Textualis type, specifically a rotunda — the formal book hand of southern Europe — as cut by Nicolas Jenson in 1474. Rotunda is a distinct branch of the blackletter family, characterised by rounder, less angular forms than Textura.
summary: A scholarly revival of Nicolas Jenson's 1474 rotunda type, designed by Riccardo Olocco for both text and display use.
description: Based on the larger of Jenson's two rotunda sizes — his 106G type used in the 1475 Decretales — the design extends the original's incomplete Latin alphabet by sourcing missing letterforms from Erhard Ratdolt's Jensonian types and Ratdolt's early Venetian numerals. OpenType historical forms include long-s ligatures (sb, sl, ss, st), a contextual round r, and a Tironian r in place of the ampersand. Unusually, the face requires no kerning pairs, a property Olocco attributes to the calligraphic origins of gothic capitals.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Rotunda Veneta as a learned revival of the Renaissance type used by Nicolas Jenson from 1474, carefully adapted for both small sizes and demanding display work. They position rotunda as the mainstream book hand of 15th-century Europe — common in theological, legal and scholastic printing well into the 1520s — and frame the project as bringing a historically significant but long-vanished type back into active use.

name: Rudolf Antiqua
classification_notes: Rudolf Antiqua is a digital revival of Koch-Antiqua (1922), which exhibits humanist serif characteristics: strongly flared stems, a small x-height with tall ascenders, an oblique stress axis, and lowercase forms traceable to Venetian Renaissance models.
summary: A careful digital revival of Rudolf Koch's Koch-Antiqua (1922), reconstructed from original Klingspor drawings and specimens by Claudio Rocha and Lucas Franco with an expanded character set and capital ligatures.
description: Rocha and Franco spent two years researching Koch's original proofs and specimens at the Klingspor Museum library before building the revival in digital format. The design preserves Koch-Antiqua's defining features: strongly flared stems, a notably small x-height that sets tall ascenders and capitals into sharp relief, and a humanist lowercase with Renaissance roots. The digital version adds accented characters, discretionary ligatures and contextual alternates, making it suited to display setting, book jackets and editorial work.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Rudolf Antiqua as a digital revival of Koch-Antiqua, a typeface first issued by the Klingspor foundry in 1922, reconstructed from enlargements of original specimens. Designed by Claudio Rocha and Lucas Franco between 2018 and 2020, it expands significantly on the original character set with accented letters, punctuation, symbols, discretionary ligatures and contextual alternates, and is intended to serve both display and short-text use in print and on screen.

name: Saffran
classification_notes: A squarish stencil sans-serif inspired by Eurostile, with deliberate stencil cuts at junctures that serve both as structural lightening and as a display feature; the geometric, near-square letterforms align it with the display category.
summary: A squarish stencil sans by Erasmo Ciufo, drawing on Eurostile's geometric structure to maximise counter space and aid legibility at small sizes.
description: Originating from a 2007 type design course at Milan Polytechnic, the design takes Eurostile's near-square skeleton as its starting point and introduces stencil cuts at stroke junctures to reduce visual weight while adding a distinctive graphic quality. Six weights from Thin to ExtraBold make it flexible across branding, pharmaceutical leaflets, packaging and book captions. The stencil detail works equally well at small text sizes and at display scale, giving the family a wider range than most stencil designs.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Saffran as a stencil sans with squarish letterforms, developed at the Milan Polytechnic in 2007 with legibility at small print sizes as the central aim. Inspired by Novarese's Eurostile, its square structure maximises counter space, while stencil cuts at junctures lighten the joins and function as a design feature in their own right. CAST suggest it for branding, packaging, pharmaceutical information leaflets, postage stamps and editorial captions.

name: Scatterplot
classification_notes: A slab serif constructed entirely from dots on a modular grid, with a variable Random axis that progressively dissolves legibility; the dot-matrix construction and brutalist weight axis place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A variable dot-matrix slab serif with a dual axis controlling weight and randomness, built on a strict modular grid with no optical corrections.
description: Designed by Giulio Galli, Scatterplot constructs each letterform from dots arranged on a geometric grid, with the Weight axis increasing dot diameter without any optical compensation and the Random axis scattering dots progressively until legibility breaks down entirely. Static fonts offer four weights crossed with four levels of randomness, from Mild to UltraWild, giving 20 preset styles alongside the variable font. Lighter settings produce the look of an old matrix printer at text sizes, while heavier and wilder combinations suit posters, titles and animated work.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Scatterplot as a variable slabserif made out of dots, recommended for animation, headlines, titles, posters and other display purposes, noting that lighter weights also work at text sizes to produce an old matrix printer effect. The family uses a double axis for Weight and Random, with static fonts offering four weights and four randomness presets, plus stylistic sets for full and empty dot variants.

name: Scotus Sans
designer: Giulio Galli, Riccardo Olocco, Luciano Perondi
classification_notes: Low-contrast humanist sans derived from the skeleton of a 15th-century Venetian roman, placing it firmly in the humanist sans tradition alongside Johnston and Gill Sans as acknowledged reference points.
summary: A humanist sans derived from the skeleton of the roman used by Octavianus Scotus in Venice in 1481, designed for long-form reading across print and screen.
description: Galli, Olocco and Perondi worked from the original Scotus roman, rationalising its letterforms into a low-contrast digital sans across nine weights and five widths, from Compressed to Extended, each with a matching italic. The 10-degree italic is slightly condensed and carries its own personality rather than functioning as a mere slant. The family draws a direct line from Johnston and Gill Sans while anchoring itself to a specific 15th-century model rather than a general humanist ideal.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Scotus Sans as a new sans based on the skeleton of the Octavianus Scotus roman of 1481, one of the most commercially successful imitations of Jenson's roman, used by over 100 printers across Europe. Designed by Galli, Olocco and Perondi, it follows the tradition of Johnston and Gill in working out the norm of plain letters, while targeting a specific historical model as its source. The result is an extensive humanistic sans that appears both contemporary and classical, suited to long reading text in print and on screen.

name: Sempione Grotesk
classification_notes: A Swiss-tradition grotesque with horizontal stroke terminations and near-uniform letter widths, placing it firmly in the neo-grotesque lineage, though enriched with ligatures and ornaments more typical of serif typography.
summary: A Swiss-tradition grotesque by Andrea Amato with a generous x-height, seven weights with cursive counterparts, and an unusually rich set of OpenType features borrowed from serif typography.
description: Developed over a decade from 2009 to 2019, the family spans Thin to Black with matching Cursive italics that echo true italic construction rather than simply slanting the upright forms. Alongside the expected lining and oldstyle figures, tabular and proportional sets, and small capitals, the family includes ligatures, ornaments and decorative features rarely found in a grotesque. A companion family, Sempione Modern, extends the system with letterforms recalling early 20th-century sanserifs and a slanted italic style.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Sempione Grotesk as a sanserif inspired by the Swiss tradition, with a large x-height suited to both publishing and advertising. They note that it brings together diverse typographic traditions, incorporating features typically associated with serif typefaces — ligatures, ornaments, and related elements — alongside hallmarks of the Swiss school such as horizontal terminations and letters of uniform width.

name: Sempione Modern
classification_notes: A Latin-script sanserif drawing on Swiss modernist and early 20th-century typographic traditions, with horizontal terminations and rational proportions that align it with humanist and neo-grotesque lineage rather than strict geometric construction.
summary: A Swiss-influenced sanserif by Andrea Amato spanning seven weights with an oblique slanted style, small caps, old-style numerals and ligatures, produced between 2009 and 2019.
description: Drawing on early 20th-century Swiss typographic rationalism, the design favours consistent stroke widths and horizontal terminals rather than calligraphic variation. The family includes a variable font counterpart and an extensive OpenType feature set covering small caps, old-style and tabular figures, discretionary ligatures and alternate letterforms. Its companion, Sempione Grotesk, offers a true cursive italic where Modern's slanted style simply inclines the upright construction unchanged.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Sempione Modern as a sanserif rooted in the Swiss tradition and early 20th-century design, with a generous x-height and clean modern proportions suited to editorial and advertising work. They note that it borrows ornamental details and ligatures more commonly found in serif typefaces, while maintaining the structural clarity of classic sanserifs. Its italic, called Slanted, preserves upright letterforms rather than adopting a cursive structure.

name: AM Serie 605
classification_notes: An all-caps geometric sans with Art Deco characteristics, originally a 1930s wood type revival. The decorative horizontal strokes, pixel-like corner cutouts and circular O forms place it firmly in the display category despite its geometric sans structure.
summary: A digital revival of a Milanese wood type from 1938-40, retaining Art Deco geometry and decorative stem extensions while adding optical refinements and OpenType alternates.
description: Originally produced by the Xilografia Milanese between 1938 and 1940, Serie 605 is an all-caps geometric sans with circular C, G, O and Q forms contrasting against otherwise condensed letters, cut corners on B, D, P and R, and horizontal strokes extending from central bars and stems. Designer Alessandro Bombieri standardised the stem extensions, introduced optical overshoots and added narrow alternates for the wide circular letters. Two stylistic sets allow switching between the distinctive extended strokes and cleaner, more restrained forms.
foundry_blurb: CAST describes AM Serie 605 as a faithful digital revival of a historic Milanese wood type, refined by Alessandro Bombieri to expand character coverage and improve optical balance while preserving the original's Art Deco character. The revival includes narrow alternates for the wide circular letters and a stylistic set removing the horizontal stem extensions for a more orderly interpretation.

name: AM Serie 610
classification_notes: An all-caps display typeface reviving a 1930s Italian wood type, with letterforms built on straight lines and circles consistent with Art Deco geometric principles. The bisected black-and-white band treatment places it firmly in the decorative display category.
summary: A digital revival of a 1930s Italian wood type by Xilografia Milanese, in which every glyph is split by a horizontal band into a black upper half and a white lower half.
description: Riccardo Olocco's revival follows the original Serie 610 closely, preserving the wood-type letterforms built on straight lines and circles, along with distinctive details such as the round apex of A, the horseshoe N and the vertical tail of Q. The bisected band treatment that defined the original is reproduced faithfully, with an optional stylistic set reinstating the thin white vertical lines between characters that appeared in physical typesetting. Special sorts, including angled, rounded, arrowed and forked band pieces for titling, are accessible via OpenType features, recreating the original supplementary material sold by the foundry.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe AM Serie 610 as an all-caps typeface whose letters and glyphs are each divided into an upper black half and a lower white half on a black band, in keeping with the Art Deco geometric tendency of the 1930s. The original was produced as wood type by Xilografia Milanese in Milan, and the digital revival by Riccardo Olocco stays very close to the source material, reproducing special sorts for front and end pieces as OpenType features and offering the original inter-character white lines as a stylistic set.

name: AM Serie 704
classification_notes: A bold all-caps condensed sans with strong thick/thin contrast and geometric, modular construction; its high contrast and condensed proportions place it firmly in the display category, reviving a late-1930s Italian wood type tradition.
summary: A digital revival of a bold condensed all-caps face originally released by Milan's Xilografia Milanese in the late 1930s, faithfully reconstructed with subtle corrections by Stefano Baldassari.
description: The original Serie 704 was a condensed sanserif distinguished by strong thick/thin contrast and modular geometric construction, with flattened inner curves and conspicuously thick terminal strokes on C, G and S. The revival, produced by Stefano Baldassari for CAST, stays close to the source while adding a horizontal bar to G, adjusting the ampersand to cap height, and expanding the character set for contemporary use. The high contrast recalls Nebiolo typefaces such as Quirinus and Hastile, situating the face in a specific tradition of Italian display type from the interwar period.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe AM Serie 704 as a bold all-caps face with geometric shapes and a modular construction, reviving a typeface originally issued by the Xilografia Milanese of Milan in the late 1930s across body sizes from 3 to 40 lines. The digital revival is described as faithful to the original, with minor corrections to G, the ampersand and the broader character set to make it fully usable.

name: AM Sirte
classification_notes: A highly condensed sans with pronounced thick/thin contrast and modular letterforms, originating from a 1940 Italian wood type. The strong contrast and idiosyncratic forms place it firmly in the display category rather than standard sans-serif.
summary: A digital revival of a 1940 Italian wood type by Xilografia di Verona, revived by Fabrizio Falcone with regularised contrast and unified rounded forms.
description: The original Sirte was a condensed wood display type produced by Xilografia di Verona around 1940, characterised by strong thick/thin contrast, wide-open apertures and idiosyncratic letterforms including rounded E, M and N. Fabrizio Falcone's revival for CAST standardises the original's inconsistencies, bringing coherence to the interplay of rounded shapes and sharp angles without erasing its distinctive personality. Part of CAST's Modernisti series, it works at headline scale where its compressed proportions and high contrast can be properly read.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe AM Sirte as a highly condensed sans with unusually strong thick/thin contrast, built from repeated modular units with wide-open apertures in both upper and lowercase. The revival is based on a wood type originally released by Xilografia di Verona around 1940; Falcone standardised the excessive irregularities of the original while preserving its idiosyncratic letterforms.

name: Sole Sans
classification_notes: Inspired by early 19th-century sanserifs, Sole Sans draws on the grotesque tradition with contrast at arch-to-stem junctions that gives it a primitive, proto-grotesque character distinct from neutral modernist sans designs.
summary: A large newspaper sans commissioned for Il Sole 24 Ore, built across five widths and nine weights with early 19th-century grotesque details at arch-to-stem junctions.
description: Designed by Riccardo Olocco and Luciano Perondi for the Italian financial daily Il Sole 24 Ore, where it has been in use since June 2018 alongside the companion Sole Serif. The family spans five widths from ExtraCondensed to Extended, each running across nine weights from Thin to Black with matching italics, making it suited to everything from dense headline setting to small diagrams and tabular data. Contrast at the junctions between arches and stems, drawn from early 19th-century sanserif models, gives the design a raw, slightly brutal texture that distinguishes it from more neutral contemporary grotesques.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Sole Sans as a newspaper sanserif workhorse spanning ultra condensed to extended widths and thin to black weights, originally commissioned for the leading Italian financial newspaper Il Sole 24 Ore. They highlight its contrast at arch-to-stem junctions as a feature borrowed from early 19th-century sanserifs, lending the design what they call a brutal and primitive character.

name: Sole Serif
classification_notes: Rooted in Francesco Griffo's Renaissance romans, Sole Serif combines inclined axis, calligraphic terminations and Renaissance proportions with a high x-height and wide italic optimised for newspaper printing — placing it firmly in the humanist serif tradition.
summary: A newspaper serif by Luciano Perondi drawing on Griffo's Renaissance romans, built across a comprehensive system of optical sizes from Small Caption to BigDisplay.
description: Designed for the demands of newspaper printing, Sole Serif bridges daily press and book typography through its big x-height and wide italic on one side and its inclined axis, calligraphic terminations and Renaissance proportions on the other. The family spans eight optical size grades and a Hairline cut, covering well over a hundred styles with weights from Light to Black. In 2018 it was chosen by Guardian Media Group to revamp The Observer, the oldest Sunday paper in the world.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Sole Serif as a newspaper face with features relating to book typography, inspired by Francesco Griffo's romans and adapted to withstand the rough conditions of newspaper printing. They highlight its big x-height, short ascenders and roundish wide italic alongside more refined details — inclined axis, calligraphic terminations and Renaissance proportions — that evoke the book rather than the daily paper. The family was used to revamp The Observer in 2018.

name: Sole Serif Rounded
classification_notes: A bracketed serif with rounded terminals and lighter joints, designed explicitly as a display face for newspaper and magazine headlines. Its optical size range and shortened serifs place it firmly in the display serif category.
summary: A rounded display cut of CAST's Sole Serif family, built across four optical sizes and six weights for newspaper and magazine headlines.
description: Derived from Sole Serif Display, this cut introduces rounded terminals, lighter joints and shorter bracketed serifs to produce a softer, more distinctive character at large sizes. The four optical sizes, Subhead, Headline, Display and BigDisplay, each carry six weights from Light to Black, giving art directors fine control over weight and scale. Suited to editorial headlines, posters and packaging where a serif needs presence without sharpness.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Sole Serif Rounded as a new cut of their most successful newspaper typeface, distinguished from Sole Serif Display by rounded edges, lighter joints and shorter bracketed serifs. They position it as a display face delivering the best results on magazine and newspaper headlines, as well as packaging, posters and similar applications, available in six weights across four optical sizes.

name: Sorella
classification_notes: Drawn from 18th-century engraved calligraphy with Fleischmann-like serif forms, sharp serifs and high thick/thin contrast in the Display size. The open counters, unconnected strokes and bulbous terminals place it firmly in the transitional tradition with a distinctly calligraphic character.
summary: A seriffed family in five weights across three optical sizes, drawn from engraved 18th-century calligraphy by Angela Baroni with Fleischmann-like capitals and a 20-degree italic.
description: Designed by Luis Castellón and sourced from Baroni's copper-plate engraving in a 1740 Venetian book of hours, the design predates Fleischmann's own adoption of the forms he is credited with. Soft, slightly compact proportions sit alongside sharp serifs, bulbous terminals and deliberately unconnected strokes in letters such as E, F, e and g, lending the text an open and airy quality. Thirty styles span Caption, Text and Display optical sizes with matching italics, small caps and four numeral styles, and the family is also available as a variable font.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Sorella as a seriffed roman and italic with a large x-height suited to headlines, body text and brand identities. They note its origins in Luis Castellón's research into Angela Baroni's engraved calligraphy from the 1740 Venetian Beatae Mariae Virginis officium, which Baroni engraved before Fleischmann himself popularised those capital forms. Castellón's initial designs were awarded the highest honours at the Experts Type Design Course in Antwerp in 2022.

name: Subiaco
classification_notes: Subiaco traces its lineage directly to the hybrid proto-roman of Sweynheym and Pannartz (1465), combining formal humanist script traits with calligraphic qualities; this places it firmly in the humanist serif tradition, predating the fully developed roman but distinct from gothic or blackletter forms.
summary: A revival of a revival, tracing the letterforms of the Ashendene Press back to the first books printed in Italy in 1465, produced using Metafont by Claudio Vincoletto.
description: The typeface reconstructs the Ashendene Press Subiaco type of around 1900, which was itself drawn from the proto-roman cut by Sweynheym and Pannartz at the Benedictine abbey of Subiaco. Vincoletto used Metafont to define the ductus of a virtual pen, overlaying grids on photographic enlargements of printed pages to establish geometric coordinates for each letterform. The result carries low contrast, long ascenders and descenders, blunt exit strokes and a substantial set of OpenType features including ligatures, scribal abbreviations and historical alternates that allow typographers to move between the two source revivals.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Subiaco as a celebrated book face from history now fully adapted to the digital environment, reviving the Ashendene Press type which itself masterfully revived the hybrid proto-roman used by Sweynheym and Pannartz in the first books printed in Italy in 1465. Vincoletto employed Metafont in an unusual way, using parametrisation to create homogeneity between letterforms rather than defining parametric equations across all design variables, and added a modular set of OpenType features to give typographers freedom in mimicking both the original Subiaco type and the Ashendene revival.

name: Swordfish
classification_notes: An all-caps geometric sans designed explicitly for display use, drawing on 1920s–30s avant-garde letterforms with varied proportions and a jumping baseline feature derived from jazz record cover typography. The display orientation and OpenType jumping-baseline feature push it firmly into display rather than general geometric sans territory.
summary: An all-caps geometric display face by Giulio Galli that marries 1920s avant-garde letterform proportions with a jumping baseline borrowed from 1950s jazz record cover typography.
description: Drawing on typefaces such as Erbar-Grotesk, Futura and Kabel, as well as their 1970s phototype successors like ITC Plaza, Swordfish pairs varied-proportion geometric capitals with an OpenType jumping-baseline feature that animates the text in a manner typical of bebop-era album cover lettering. A suite of swash capitals, discretionary ligatures including nested letterforms, and wide Latin language coverage extend its versatility for signs, posters and menus. The single Regular weight keeps the offering focused entirely on display and titling applications.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Swordfish as a contemporary take on the geometric sanserifs of the avant-garde era and their 1970s successors, uniting varied-proportion geometric letterforms with a jumping baseline typical of early jazz album covers. Designed by Giulio Galli, the face includes OpenType features such as the jumping baseline, swashed capitals and ligatures with small nested letters, making it suitable for display applications including signs, posters, menus and album covers.

name: AM Tirana
classification_notes: Condensed geometric sans derived from a 1938-41 Italian wood type, characterised by low contrast, large x-height, open apertures and sharp geometric detailing including lenticular counters and squared or angled spurs.
summary: A digital revival of an Italian wood type from c.1938-41, drawn by Leo Philp with lenticular counters, sharp spurs and exceedingly open apertures.
description: Based on Tirana, a condensed geometric wood type produced by Xilografia Italiana of Badia Polesine, AM Tirana preserves the source material's low contrast, large x-height and characteristic open C and S alongside pointed A and N vertices. The design is set apart by its treatment of joints and spurs, which close at sharp angles or are squared off, and by its lemon-slice and lenticular counters. OpenType features reflect the original type's conventions, including raised dashes and compact parentheses for all-caps setting, plus stylistic alternates for the C, G, 6, 9 and ampersand.
foundry_blurb: CAST describes AM Tirana as a condensed geometric sans that is rich in colour, with sharp details, low contrast and very open apertures. The design draws on classic modernist lettering traditions and is defined by its squared or sharply closed spurs and joints, along with lenticular and lemon-slice counters. The wood type original was produced by Xilografia Italiana most likely between 1938 and 1941, and a slanted variant was sold under the name Corsica.

name: AM Tobruk
classification_notes: A condensed sans-serif with pronounced thick/thin contrast and rounded corners derived from a c.1940 wood type original; its geometric, streamlined character and period-specific detailing place it firmly in art deco display territory.
summary: A digital revival of a c.1940 Xilografia di Verona wood type, preserving the Art Deco character of the original while rationalising its more erratic letterforms.
description: Originally cut as wood type by Xilografia di Verona around 1940, this condensed sans carries an unusually strong thick/thin contrast alongside rounded corners and a small x-height that together mark its Art Deco origins clearly. Andy Anzollitto's revival refines the letterform construction for digital use, introducing consistent terminal treatment and redrawn glyphs while retaining idiosyncrasies such as the vertical S, the oblique-stemmed M and N, and the elongated stems of p and q. The result is a single-weight display face with broad Latin language support, suited to headline and poster work where period warmth is wanted without raw historical roughness.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Tobruk as a condensed sanserif with rounded corners and a strong thick/thin contrast, with each character featuring just one thick stroke. The small x-height and streamlined letterforms reflect a clear Art Deco influence, underlined by distinctive details including a vertical s and S, lowercase-style capitals M and N with oblique thin stems, and stems of p and q that rise above the x-height. Originally issued as a wood type by Xilografia di Verona around 1940, the digital revival by Andy Anzollitto refines the geometry and normalises the more inconsistent lowercase forms.

name: AM Toscana
classification_notes: A revival of a 1934 Italian wood type, AM Toscana is a semi-condensed italic sans with wedge-tapered stems and vernacular letterforms, placing it firmly in the display category rather than a functional text sans.
summary: A revival of a 1934 Italian wood type, distinguished by systematically wedge-tapered stems and vernacular semi-condensed letterforms, designed by Ian Farnam.
description: Released in 2025 by CAST, AM Toscana revives a wood type originally issued by Xilografia Meneghello e Belluzzo around 1934, itself possibly derived from Wedge Gothic, an American BB&S cut of 1893. Ian Farnam rationalised the weight and proportions, increased the x-height for contemporary legibility, and redesigned the round letters to echo the characteristic tapering of the stems. The result is a single-style italic with tall ascenders, short descenders and a spiky, top-heavy rhythm suited to poster and editorial display work.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe AM Toscana as an eclectic wood type characterised by vernacular letterforms: a spiky semi-condensed italic sans with low thick-thin contrast and the defining feature of systematic wedge-like tapering of stems from top to bottom across all glyphs. The original was released by Xilografia Meneghello e Belluzzo in six body sizes around 1934 and may have been loosely inspired by Wedge Gothic, cut by Barnhart Brothers & Spindler in 1893.

name: AM Tripoli
classification_notes: An all-caps geometric sans with expressionist characteristics drawn from early 20th-century Central European poster lettering, featuring abrupt angular strokes and sharp triangular details that place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A digital revival of an Italian wood type from the 1930s, retaining the expressionist angularity and hand-cut character of the original Xilografia Italiana specimen.
description: Originally produced as wood type by Xilografia Italiana around the time of Mussolini's mass settlement of Libya, the typeface carries a charged historical context behind its stark geometric forms. Bombieri's revival rationalises the original's flaws for the digital environment while preserving the abrupt angles, flattened arches and sharp triangular strokes that give it its raw, poster-cut character. With a single weight and no italics, it is purpose-built for display use where texture and historical weight matter.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe AM Tripoli as a dark, uppercase sanserif with a strong expressionist feel, with geometric letterforms featuring flattened arches, tiny counters and sharp edges. They note its origins as a wood type produced by Xilografia Italiana, probably shortly after the company's founding in 1934, and draw comparisons to the expressionist paper-cut lettering style exemplified by Rudolf Koch's Neuland and Viennese artists such as Oskar Kokoschka.

name: TXC Pearl
classification_notes: An uppercase-only condensed sans with Jugendstil ornamental inflections, designed for display use. Its historical reference to American wood type and Art Nouveau lettering places it firmly in the display category despite its grotesque structural underpinning.
summary: An uppercase condensed sans by Andy Anzollitto that fuses American wood type traditions with Jugendstil lettering sourced from 19th-century German-Texan newspapers.
description: Designed as the first release in the Texanische type collection, TXC Pearl draws on specimens from German typefounders including Berthold and Schelter & Giesecke, filtered through advertisements in newspapers like Die Freie Presse Für Texas. The design is caps-only with condensed proportions, slightly squarish round forms and gently flared stems that hint at serifs without committing to them. Distinctive details include flat apexes on A and M, elevated central bars on E and F, and small bowls on P and R that break quietly from grotesque convention.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe TXC Pearl as the first in Andy Anzollitto's Texanische series, a typographic study of the Texas Hill Country inspired by 19th-century German immigrant communities who settled the region. The typeface draws on historical newspaper advertisements, German type foundry specimens and carved stone lettering, positioning type design as an investigative tool for exploring place and social history.

name: AM Udine
classification_notes: An all-caps, monolinear, modular sans with narrow proportions and geometric construction rooted in Art Deco wood-type tradition; its single weight and display-only character place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A digital revival of a 1933 Nebiolo wood-type design, reconstructing the all-caps, monolinear geometry of the original with careful optical refinements by Michele Patanè.
description: Originally produced as a wood type by the Turinese Nebiolo foundry and first catalogued in 1933, Udine is an all-caps sans whose entire alphabet is constructed from the modular forms of H and O. Patanè's revival tightens horizontal stroke weights and smooths curve transitions to compensate for on-screen optical distortion while preserving the rigid, analogue stiffness of the source material. OpenType features reinstate the original negative-shape characters and decorative title pieces, making the revival functionally complete.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe AM Udine as a highly accurate digital revival of a Nebiolo all-caps modular sanserif with clear Art Deco influences, noting its close kinship with Nebiolo's better-known Neon family and suggesting Udine was likely an earlier developmental stage of that typeface. The designer refined stroke weights and curve transitions for digital use while retaining the analogue stiffness of the 1933 original, and reinstated the negative-shape characters and decorative pieces via OpenType features.

name: Valnera
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a low-contrast humanist serif with angular construction, broad-pen influenced terminals and simil-slab serifs; its calligraphic origins and ink-trap-like crotches place it firmly in the humanist serif tradition.
summary: An angular, low-contrast humanist serif by Riccardo De Franceschi, drawing on broad-pen calligraphy and the spiky character of 1970s photocomposition faces such as Cartier and ITC Mendoza.
description: Sharp connections between stems and arches, flat horizontal curves and ink-trap-like crotches give the typeface an interrupted, almost fragmented counter construction reminiscent of Dwiggins's m-formula. Eight weights with matching italics cover text and display use, while three additional subfamilies -- SemiRandom, Random and the headline-only Monster -- introduce glyph variation to break up the regularity of typeset text. The family is also available as variable fonts and supports an extensive Latin Extended character set across dozens of languages.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Valnera as a low-contrast humanist serif of distinctly angular design, suited to both display and text sizes. Its simil-slab serifs, open counters and flat horizontal curves show the influence of a broad pen, while sharp crotch cuts act as ink traps. The design evokes the calligraphic spirit of Oldřich Menhart and the 1970s photocomposition feeling of Cartier and ITC Mendoza. First drawn in 2010--2011 by Riccardo De Franceschi, it was reviewed and completed in collaboration with CAST, with a Random version developed to add variety to text setting.

name: AM Vittoria
classification_notes: An all-caps condensed geometric sans with pronounced thick/thin contrast and distinctive Art Déco letterforms; the condensed proportions, almond-shaped O and inverted B bowls place it firmly in display territory rather than functional sans-serif.
summary: A digital revival of a 1940 Neapolitan metal typeface, restoring its Art Déco geometry with contemporary refinements for poster and signage use.
description: Originally cast by Fonderia Tipografica Meridionale Armando de Luca of Naples around 1940, the design carries hallmark Art Déco traits: a detached crossbar on A, inverted bowls on B, an almond-shaped O and wide central bars on E and F. Muk Monsalve redistributed contrast on several letters, redrew numerals and introduced monolinear contextual alternates for C, G and S to resolve inconsistencies inherent in the original. An inline version of the full character set is included as a stylistic set via OpenType.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe AM Vittoria as a condensed all-caps geometric sans with pronounced thick/thin contrast, reviving a metal typeface from around 1940. Designer Muk Monsalve adjusted spacing for large-scale applications, rebalanced contrast on letters such as A and M, redrew numerals, and designed diacritics from scratch, while retaining defining features like the almond O. Monolinear contextual alternates for C, G and S were added to address contrast inconsistencies, and an inline version is available as a stylistic set.

name: Voland
classification_notes: Drawn from Baskerville's Double Pica specimen of 1757, Voland is a transitional serif with deliberately reduced thick/thin contrast and an increased x-height for text use.
summary: A text serif rooted in Baskerville's 1757 Double Pica, originally cut for the Voland publishing house in 2010 and updated with a variable font and expanded character set.
description: Designed by Luciano Perondi for the Italian Voland publishing house, this transitional serif deliberately restrains its thick/thin contrast to match the page colour of Simoncini Garamond, a benchmark of Italian book publishing since the 1960s. Five weights run from Book to ExtraBold with matching italics, small caps in every weight, oldstyle and tabular figures, and a variable font version. The increased x-height and open counters make it comfortable at text sizes in both book and magazine settings.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Voland as a seriffed typeface for books and magazines, originally created for the independent Voland publishing house in 2010 and now available with a new character set. Inspiration for the regular weights came from Baskerville's Double Pica in his 1757 type specimen, though a close imitation was intentionally avoided. Distinctive features include an elegant fox-like Q tail, reduced thick/thin contrast, and an x-height modelled on Simoncini Garamond to give the page an even colour at text sizes.

name: Xanti Typewriter
classification_notes: A fixed-width typeface with geometric Bauhaus construction; serifs disappear progressively as weight increases, placing it firmly in the monospaced geometric tradition.
summary: A nine-weight monospaced family drawn from Xanti Schawinsky's 1930s Olivetti logo, with serifs that dissolve as weight increases.
description: Designed by Gianluca Sandrone and produced between 2020 and 2021, the family takes its formal starting point from the geometric logo Schawinsky created for Olivetti in the mid-1930s. Eight weights from Hairline to ExtraBold each come with a matching italic, and the progressive loss of serifs through the weight range is a deliberate structural decision rather than an afterthought. OpenType features include lining and old style figures, slashed zero, alternate letterforms and a variable font version, making it suitable for coding environments, editorial work and packaging alike.
foundry_blurb: CAST describe Xanti Typewriter as a monospaced type family rooted in the geometric Olivetti logo designed by Xanti Schawinsky in the mid-1930s. They note that as letter weight increases, almost all glyphs shed their serifs, a decision that allowed the family to function across a wide range of applications from packaging to editorial design and coding. The family ships with lining and old style numerals, slashed zero and alternates.
